lines: You sit by a Witherwood pine for a moment.
You realize you cannot remember what pine smells like.
Atrél has it now. He has set it on the altar between the others.
You write your save. As you do, you try to think of your mother saying your name.
You cannot hear it anymore. The sound is gone. The shape of her voice is gone.
Atrél holds it. He is gentle. You did not give it; it left you.
You stand at the Crossroads and try to remember which road you used to take
before this one. You cannot. You know there was another. The shape is missing.
Atrél receives the missing shape. He sets it with the others. He is full now.
You stop in the south aisle. There was a song you used to know. You hum the start.
The next note is gone. The song closes itself inside you, like a door at the end of a hall.
Atrél has it. The hall is shorter now. It still has rooms.
You write your save. You realize you no longer remember the name of the first
child who chose you back. The face is also going. The whole afternoon is going.
Atrél catches the afternoon as it falls. He has soft hands for that kind of thing.
You are reading. You reach for a word you have loved your whole life.
It is not there. You know its shape. You cannot say it. Atrél has the word.
It is on the altar between the others. He whispers it once for safekeeping.
Something hot brushes your left hand as the fight ends. You do not see what.
By the time you look, it is gone. The skin is white-pink where it should not be.
The hand will work. The hand will hurt. The hand is yours, in a different way now.
(-3 Max HP, permanently.)
You touch your temple, after. There is a white hair where there was none.
It is short. It is a child's grey. It will not change back. You leave it.
Atrél, who keeps such things, considers it an unbillable expense.
Something opened the skin under your jaw and the skin closed itself fast.
You did not notice when. The scar is a small ledge. Your fingers will find it
for the rest of your life. The next thing that swings at your face will glance.
(+1 Defense, permanently.)
After the fight, a back tooth is loose. You did not notice the blow that did it.
You touch it with your tongue. It will not go back. You will lose it later.
Atrél, who collects the small ones, has gentle hands for teeth.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently — you sleep less well now.)
Your hands have finally gone hard where they need to be hard.
You did not notice. You notice now. Holding the weapon does not hurt today.
It will not hurt again. Your grip will tell the next thing you swing at.
(+1 Attack, permanently.)
You walk down the choir's nave and realize your right leg is slow.
It did not used to be. Something at the gauntlet took it. You did not notice.
The leg will work. The leg will be slower. You will sleep less to outwalk it.
(-2 Max Stamina, permanently. The Pall does not need to know.)
You are nearly down. You feel — and you know without seeing — that the Pall
has just looked at you, briefly, from somewhere very far away. The look is gone.
But you are now sure of something you did not used to be sure of. You are real to it.
You read. As you do, something in you wonders if you have been told a story.
Maybe Atrél is the kingdom's coping mechanism. Maybe the small altar is theatre.
You do not know what to do with the thought. The thought stays. It has joined you.
Something about this place — the air, the height of the ceiling, the unsung hymn —
decides for you that defending here is the wrong shape of action. You cannot do it.
You will not. Your body will refuse. The choir does not let you stand still.
(You can no longer choose Defend in the Choir.)
Walking away from the wolf you just killed, you become certain — without proof —
that this animal used to wear a collar. You can see the wear-line in its fur.
You will see it in every wolf you fight from now on. You will not be able to un-see it.
You crest a switchback and freeze. You cannot say why. Your legs simply
decide they are not going further today. You sit. The grey snow falls on you.
You will climb tomorrow. Today you cannot. The fear is yours now. It does not leave.
You walk into the Reach knowing — without checking — that the Engineer will
smile at you today. She does. You do not know why you knew. The knowing stays.
Atrél has filed it. It is the kind of thing he likes to keep.
The cat by the hearth, the same blue cat, has shifted. She is sitting closer to
where you usually sit. By a hand's width. She did not used to. She does now.
The cat will keep sitting at this distance. The distance is yours. Atrél will not have it.
A wolf — not the one you just killed; one watching from the trees — sees you.
She does not move. She knows you now. She will not be at the next Witherwood
fight you have. She will not be at any of them. You and she have decided.
(+1 Defense in the Witherwood, permanently. One wolf has chosen you.)
The Small Un, today, looks up when you walk in. They do not look up for everyone.
They have decided that you are someone. They will not forget. Atrél will not get
to forget you for them, either. You and the Small Un are written into each other.
The third tree from the road north has shifted very slightly. It is leaning
toward you. It is not the wind. The tree has been a tree a long time and
has decided. You did not earn this. It is yours anyway. Carry it gently.
After the fight, you pass Halna at her log. She does not look up. She does say,
to no one, that the next meat she dresses she will save you a cut. She means it.
The next time you arrive at her clearing, there will be a strip of meat on a stone.
(Halna will leave you a free Health Potion the next time you arrive.)
The Atrél-marker — Tessa's marker, you know now — registers your step the way
soil registers rain. It is not a magic. It is a smaller thing. The marker knows you.
When you pass it again, the stone will be a degree warmer than the air around it.
The cat by the hearth does not look at you today. She will not look at you again
this character. You did something. You do not know what. Atrél did not take this;
the cat decided. The cat is older than Atrél. The cat is allowed her decisions.
Halna will not nod at you when you pass her fallen pine. Not today. Not again.
The nod was a small thing. It is gone. You do not know which of the wolves she
feeds is the one that decided this. Halna does. She will not tell you.
You save. You realize, as you close the page, that you have not had a dream in
many nights. You used to. The dreams have left you. They will not come back.
Atrél has them all in a stack now. He has bound them. They are very neat.
The Insomniac is still counting on her beam. She is not counting you, today.
She used to. You used to be a dark mark. Today you are no mark at all.
She will not start again. The counting goes on without you. It is alright.
The Bellward's bell, that has been ringing your every hour since you arrived,
stops finding you. You hear it ring; the sound passes through you. It does not
ask. The Bellward has, somewhere in his fog, taken you off his list. You are sorry.
You realize, in the Gullet's wet, that you no longer have your left glove.
You do not remember where it went. The Older Hand will find it, eventually,
and set it among the one-of-each. You will not be there. You have walked on.
Your hand brushes a pebble on the path. It is warmer than the air. You pick it
up. It does not cool. You put it in your pocket. Atrél has not asked for it.
It is yours. It will not be in anyone else's pocket, this run or the next.
(A Warm Pebble has been added to your inventory.)
There is a small wooden button in the silt at your boot. Worn, bored through.
You pick it up. It was someone's, once. It is yours, in the smallest possible way.
Atrél holds the someone. The button is for you to carry. He has not asked for it.
On the path between the bakery and the Small Un's, a thread of dyed wool —
green, almost grey now. It must be the Small Un's. You pick it up. You knot
it once into the corner of your cloak. The third knot is for warmth, not show.
Tucked under a stone where you sat to read, a coin. Karst minting. The edges
soft. Someone's, once. Yours now. You will give it back, one day, if you find
where it goes. You will not find where it goes. You will carry it anyway.
On the south aisle's bench, where the column eats the word 'name,' a single
white feather. You take it. It weighs less than it should. The Choir does not
miss it. The feather is yours. Carry it. It is one small thing the Pall left.
Kerris's pack is open by the oven. There is a crust in it, set aside. He has
left it for you. He will not say so. He will not look up when you take it.
It will keep. It is from the bread Mira's recipe was for. It is enough for once.
(Kerris's Crust added — a one-use heal of 25 HP.)
You realize, three steps after the crossing, that the knot you tied in your cloak
for warmth is gone. The river took it. The river is patient. It tries everything.
Atrél has the knot now. He has set it carefully. It is shaped like a small house.
Counting your purse in passing, you find the smallest copper is gone.
You do not remember when it left. It was the one your father gave you,
or someone's father. You did not notice the absence until just now.
Atrél holds it. He holds everyone's smallest one. He is, in this, very rich.
You hum, while saving, a tune you have hummed all your life. The third note
is missing today. You try again. Still missing. You will not get it back.
Atrél has the third note. The rest is yours. The song still works, in pieces.
You taste the wet of the Gullet on your lips. There is no salt in it. You realize
you no longer know what salt tastes like. You knew this morning. You do not now.
Atrél has it. It will season nothing of his. He does not season. He only keeps.
Your left glove is gone. You do not remember when. The hand will be cold.
The hand will harden. The hand will be slower to block, until it learns.
Atrél has not asked for the glove. The Older Hand will find it. Eventually.
(-1 Defense, permanently — a small cold has gotten into you.)
You reach for the word kindness while reading. You cannot find it. You know its
shape; you know what it means; you cannot say it. Atrél has the word itself.
He has bowed. The kindness is still in you. It will work without its name.
Reading at Halna's stump, you decide — without saying so aloud — that you will
come back to it. Tomorrow, or the day after. Halna does not need you to. You
have decided. Promises are small. They are also kept. You are someone who keeps.
You read the wall behind one of the five chairs. You decide, quietly, that if
you reach the Summit you will say their name aloud. Just the one. You do not
say which. The wall does not need to know which. The wall has heard, anyway.
You walk past the hearth. You decide you will write a line in the book before
you leave this village this run. You do not need to tell the hearth-keeper.
She will know when you do it. She will not ask what you wrote. She will keep it.
You decide, at a small grave you have read, that you will lay one flower here
before this run ends. One. Small. The flower does not have to be impressive.
It only has to be set. You will set one. The dead get every flower offered them.
Walking past the bakery, you decide you will remember Kerris's name no matter
what Atrél takes from you. You write the name in your cloak's seam, in pencil.
It will smudge. You will write it again. Some names you decide to keep yourself.
You decide — quietly, walking past his altar — that next time you pass here,
you will set one small grief down. You know which. You will not name it now.
Atrél will be here. He has been here. He will accept it the way he accepts all of them.
You realize, on the climb, that you did not go back to Halna's stump. You
promised yourself you would. You did not. There is no harm done; it is a stump.
Halna sets fresh bread on it tonight, the way she sets it every night.
The breaking is small. It is yours. Atrél does not take broken promises. They stay.
(-2 Max HP, permanently. You will feel this. You meant well, once.)
You stand at the Summit. You forgot which name. You promised to say one. You
cannot. It is, of course, exactly the one Atrél will keep for you, now. The
wall in the Sealed Chamber will not mind. It has heard people forget before.
(-1 Attack, permanently. You meant to remember. You did not.)
You are bleeding. You remember, abruptly, the flower you promised to lay.
You do not have a flower. You do not have time. You will not have a flower
again this run. The promise is broken. The grave is the same grave. It waits.
You save. You realize Kerris's name is no longer in your cloak's seam. It must
have smudged out. You meant to write it again. You did not. Atrél has it now.
Kerris would forgive you. You will not ask him to. You will keep the silence.
You realize, far from the village, that you did not write the line. The book is
still on the hearth. It is still open. Someone else will write the next one.
The hearth-keeper does not mind. You mind. The minding is yours. It does not leave.
(-1 Max Stamina, permanently. The sleeplessness of small things.)
You save. You realize — too late, again — that you did not bring the grief to
Atrél's altar this run. You meant to. You meant well. Atrél's hands are open
anyway, in the gentle way they are always open. He is patient. You are not the
first promise-breaker. He has held this kind of forgetting many times. He will hold yours.
At the edge of your vision, in the gauntlet, a face. Just a face. You did not
see whose. You did not have time to look. The face is gone. You know it was someone.
You will not know who. Atrél files the almost-seeing. It is enough for him to file.
The river is speaking. You almost hear your name in it. Not your name; close to it.
You stop to listen. The river goes back to being water. The almost-hearing stays.
It will surface at odd moments. You will not be able to say which moments. It is yours.
Walking past Kerris's oven, you almost remember a smell that is older than you.
Your grandmother, maybe. Your mother. You do not have the smell — only the
almost-having. Atrél has the smell. The almost is yours. The almost is enough.
You almost understand what Atrél is doing. You almost see the shape of it. You
do not, in the end. The almost-understanding stays with you. It is a kind of
knowing. It is not the knowing. You will carry the almost. You will not say so.
On the climb, your foot decided — once, between two steps — to turn around.
You did not. You climbed. The decision is in you, though. You almost did. You
know you almost did. Atrél has the almost. The Pall does not have it yet.
You are nearly down. The grey, for half a breath, shifts in a shape that looks
like an old man bowing. You blink. The grey is the grey again. Hunger never bows.
The almost-bowing is yours. It is a heavy thing. Atrél nods at you. He does not bow.
